provenance.source_corpus: Movement and Spread of a Founding Population of Reintroduced Elk ( Cervus elaphus ) in Ontario, Canada

source_spans.text: We believe that the hard release methodology likely contributed to the dispersion of elk following release in our study.

Calves dispersed signiﬁcantly shorter distances than adults

nodes.name: Predation, Allee effects, and dispersal capabilities

source_spans.text: Predation, Allee effects, and dispersal capabilities are important considerations for reintroductions. Such factors can depress initial rates of population growth and spread

nodes.name: Initial rates of population growth and spread

source_spans.text: Predation, Allee effects, and dispersal capabilities are important considerations for reintroductions. Such factors can depress initial rates of population growth and spread
During the ﬁrst 1 1/2 years, lack of release-site ﬁdelity and high dispersal coupled with animal-human conﬂicts and mortalities likely contributed to an initial lag in population growth.
During the ﬁrst 1 1/2 years, lack of release-site ﬁdelity and high dispersal coupled with animal-human conﬂicts and mortalities likely contributed to an initial lag in population growth.
During the ﬁrst 1 1/2 years, lack of release-site ﬁdelity and high dispersal coupled with animal-human conﬂicts and mortalities likely contributed to an initial lag in population growth.
During the ﬁrst 1 1/2 years, lack of release-site ﬁdelity and high dispersal coupled with animal-human conﬂicts and mortalities likely contributed to an initial lag in population growth.
During the ﬁrst 1 1/2 years, lack of release-site ﬁdelity and high dispersal coupled with animal-human conﬂicts and mortalities likely contributed to an initial lag in population growth.
Ryckman et al. (2009) also noted in Ontario that extended conditioning periods for elk prior to release promoted philopatry.
Ryckman et al. (2009) also noted in Ontario that extended conditioning periods for elk prior to release promoted philopatry.
The social cohesion and site tenacity of Tule elk reintroduced in California, for example, was attributed largely to holding animals in small pens for 3–6 months prior to release
The social cohesion and site tenacity of Tule elk reintroduced in California, for example, was attributed largely to holding animals in small pens for 3–6 months prior to release
The primary causes of known mortalities in our study were emaciation, collision with automobiles, and illegal shooting.
